chapters.title: The mindset behind the game
chapters.text:
Donald Trump has always cared more about sounding good than being right. For him, truth is a soft gray area, and he does not seem to mind when fact checkers call him out.

Back in 2015, a friend of the author shared a dinner with Donald and Melania Trump. Couples drifted into side chats during the meal. One of the women told Melania her accent was lovely and asked where she was from. Melania said Slovenia. Donald cut in from his own chat and said, "Tell her Austria. It sounds better."

There was also his 2013 post aimed at Mark Cuban. Trump bragged he had won 18 club championships. A check of the claim showed that all 18 came from golf courses he owned and had just opened. Even then, 16 of those titles were flat lies, and the other two could not be confirmed.

Play golf with a man to see his real character, as P. G. Wodehouse once wrote.

chapters.title: A long line of golfing presidents
chapters.text:
Many past US presidents loved the game. The White House can feel like a gilded prison with butlers, and the chief resident cannot leave without a week of planning and a squad of agents in tow. Golf was a perfect release.

William Howard Taft once made the president of Chile wait while he teed off. Woodrow Wilson took up the game on doctor advice and got hooked, going so far as to paint his golf balls black so he could play in the snow. Warren Harding was a joy on the course and died of heart failure in San Francisco, his favorite golf city. Calvin Coolidge was not much of a player. Franklin Roosevelt may have been the most gifted golfing president. He won medals as a teen and the Campobello club title, and he left a public legacy that included Bethpage, the New York course that hosted the 2019 PGA Championship. Dwight Eisenhower loved the game and was a close friend of Arnold Palmer. He had a putting green put in near the Oval Office so he could practice. Lyndon Johnson used golf to win political support, and legend has it he gathered the votes to pass the Civil Rights Act on the course.

Later presidents joined in. Nixon played to an 18 handicap in office and a 12 after he resigned. Gerald Ford was not much of a player. George H. Bush was an 18 handicap who could finish in two hours. Bill Clinton took his time on the course, six hours a round was nothing, and he was good company for the slow walk. George W. Bush played to a 15 and was a friend of Ben Crenshaw, who said the Bushes do not cheat. Obama played to an 11, did not cheat, and liked private rounds with sportswriters. Donald Trump rounds out the list.

Trump does more than play. He builds, buys, owns, runs, sues over, lies about, and brags about his courses. Golf shapes the people he knows, the deals he cuts, the favors he hands out, the trouble he gets into, the places he goes, the money he makes and loses, and even the views in his head.

chapters.title: How the cheat was born
chapters.text:
Donald Trump picked up golf with real hunger while at the Wharton School at Penn. He played at Cobbs Creek, a public course full of gamblers, hustlers, and laid off steel workers. The course was a short drive from campus.

At Cobbs Creek, every moment around a round came with a bet. If one player could think up a wager, another would take it. Trump learned the game in that stew. He played his friends and then the hustlers. He bet, won some, and lost some.

At Cobbs Creek, he built the swing that sends the ball flat along the ground, and he soaked up the hustler mind set.

Several people who know his game were asked to rate it. His longtime caddie put him at a solid 7. Pro player Brad Faxon called him a legit 10. Four time major winner Ernie Els said 8 or 9. LPGA great Annika Sörenstam said 9 or 10.

Donald Trump says he is a 2.8. So Donald Trump cheats at golf. As a member of Winged Foot, caddies called him Pele because he allegedly kicked his ball back into the fairway. Dr. Lance Dodes, a Harvard psychiatrist and coauthor of The Dangerous Case of Donald Trump, has said Trump shows signs of a narcissistic personality disorder. A constant need to be the best, and a refusal to lose or look like less than the best, runs through his behavior, as Dodes put it.

If Trump cheats at golf, it follows that he cheats in business, taxes, politics, with his wife, and in his net worth, his crowd sizes, his weight, and even the height of his buildings.

In life, we are defined by the obstacles we overcome. If you cheat to get around them, you never know the thrill of beating them for real, as Rick Reilly wrote.

chapters.title: A closer look at the course claims
chapters.text:
Donald Trump has called his collection the greatest in the history of golf, a set of 14 courses by the end of 2018. The courses are nice, but they are not as great as he says.

Most of the land they sit on was bought cheap, with Trump himself once saying 10 to 15 cents on the dollar, and much of it has been in default or went bankrupt. In the 2019 to 2020 edition of Golf Digest Top 100 American Courses, none of Trump properties made the list, even when they did well in the overall ranking. Trump International in West Palm Beach sat at 178 in America. Trump Turnberry took 10th in the world. Trump International at Aberdeen was 64th worldwide.

The real top spot goes to Mike Keiser, the owner of Bandon Dunes in Oregon. All four of his courses there sit on the Top 100 list.

chapters.title: A cold welcome in Scotland
chapters.text:
Donald Trump has family roots in Scotland, and many Scots dislike him for it. There are at least two reasons.

First, his grasp of UK and island geography is weak. He once suggested Ireland was part of the UK and that Scotland was a country apart from it. On Brexit, he said Scotland took their country back. They did not. Scotland voted 62 percent against Brexit.

The other reason is golf. In 2006, Trump set out to build a luxury public resort near Balmedie, just north of Aberdeen. The site was the Menie Estate, an old shooting ranch he had picked up for 11 million dollars. The plan called for two courses, an eight story five star hotel with 450 rooms, a sports complex, 950 time share units, and 36 villas. All of this sat on a stretch of live dunes marked as a Site of Special Scientific Interest, the highest grade of protection in Scotland.

The fight became a standoff, captured in the documentary You Have Been Trumped. Today, only one of the two planned courses has been built. No hotel, no time share units, no 6,000 new jobs, and no 1.28 billion dollars for the local economy. Trump family roots in Scotland pushed back hard and won.

chapters.title: Breaking every rule of the game
chapters.text:
President Trump is famous for breaking political norms, and golf gave him decades of practice.

Golf etiquette asks players to take off their hats for the end of round handshake and in the clubhouse. Donald Trump does not.

No matter whose turn it is, Trump steps up first and hits. He hates to lose. After losing a 50 dollar bet to a member at Winged Foot, he bad mouthed the man for cashing the check instead of framing it.

Donald Trump does not walk, and that breaks a core aim of the game. He drives his cart right onto the greens, a serious breach. Caddies do not even set the bag on the green because it leaves tire marks that can steer putts off line.

Trump also drives across tee boxes and plays through groups without asking. He also pressures golf magazines to rate his courses well. He fumes when other courses rank higher or when his own get no rank at all. To explain the missing ranks, he falsely claims the policy is to spare his members any hassle.

The truth is he wants those lists, but ranking is one thing he cannot buy or bully his way into, and that drives him mad.

chapters.title: A round with the President
chapters.text:
Say you get an invite to tee off with President Trump. Here is what to expect.

First, you will play one of his own courses. Since taking office, he has played only at his own places, even passing up Winged Foot, where he is a member.

Get ready for a round like no other. Expect protesters near the gate. Because the President only plays his own courses, protests are a regular sight. When you arrive, you will be sent to the locker room to change shoes and drink Trump coffee, Trump vodka, or Trump water, all now out of business. After that, you will head to the range for warm up shots.

Trump will greet you with a 20 second pull you in handshake, his left hand on your right shoulder. Be warned. He does not practice much.

Get ready for the heavy security. As many as 60 Secret Service agents, 6 SWAT team members, and 30 carts can appear, with staff carrying the nuclear football, the assistant chief of staff, a doctor with vials of the President blood, a comms aide, a secure satellite phone, a one man portable bomb shelter, gas masks, machine guns, weapons, and a small missile, all rolling along as you play. If you look closely, you may spot four camouflaged Marines with machine guns hidden in the brush near the fence line.

Expect a fire truck, an ambulance, around ten black SUVs, police cars, and dogs staged near the club.

As play begins, you may see guests on the fairway being patted down, and you will play through them without asking. You may see the same in the dining room after the round if the President happens to be eating there.

Expect a fast moving round. Do not expect deep talk with the President. Do not expect to know anyone score. Above all, expect him to win, fair or not. He is, after all, the President of the United States.

chapters.title: From the course to the country
chapters.text:
Many of the problems and calls that have marked Donald Trump time in office can be traced back to his golf.

There are a few clear examples. In 2008, the Trump Organization pitched a plan to manage the Coco Beach Golf and Country Club, about 30 minutes from San Juan, Puerto Rico. The club was losing about 5.4 million dollars a year, and Trump promised to turn it around.

After Trump took over, losses rose to 6.3 million dollars a year, including the 600,000 dollar fee the Trump Organization pulled out.

Seven years later, and after pushing the Coco Beach Company into 33 million dollars of government loans, the Trump Organization declared bankruptcy on its piece of the project in 2015. Coco Beach Company folded, and Puerto Ricans were left holding a 33 million dollar debt.

In 2017, a fierce hurricane tore across the island and took out most of the infrastructure. Puerto Ricans went without power and water for eight months. Three thousand people lost their lives in the crisis. The federal government under Trump was nowhere to be found.

The so called travel ban on Muslim countries is another case. The plan called for blocking U.S. entry by citizens of Iran, Iraq, Libya, Somalia, Sudan, Syria, and Yemen, on the grounds of a terror threat. Americans asked why the United Arab Emirates, a known funder of the Taliban, and Saudi Arabia, the source of many terror recruits, were left off the list. Why was Indonesia, where domestic terror in a single year can kill more than all global terror combined, also left off?

Could the reason be the Trump International Golf Club Dubai and the Trump World Golf Club Dubai? Could it be his well known business ties to Saudi royals? Could it be that the Trump Organization was about to open two courses in Bali and Jakarta?

Trump ran on a Buy American, Hire American line. Yet once in office, he signed an order raising the number of H 2B temporary visa workers allowed into the United States. Could that be because the Trump Organization staffs its courses and hotels with H 2B workers?

Trump is friends with world leaders who play golf, like Japan Shinzo Abe, and cool to those who do not, like China Xi Jinping and Germany Angela Merkel. Since he took office, he has put at least five members of his own golf courses into serious roles in his administration.

Nearly every call the President makes seems to touch his wealth. There is a sense that lobbyists, special interest dealers, and foreign go betweens can win his attention by paying the entry fee at one of his clubs or by hosting events at his properties.

Past presidents gave up possible conflicts of interest before taking office. Trump kept his business ties, saying Don, Eric, and Ivanka will be running things while he serves.

chapters.title: The high cost of winning at any price
chapters.text:
Winning is good. Winning at any cost is not.

Donald Trump shows the win at all costs mind set that runs through many narcissists. In his drive to win and stay on top, he shows the flaws that put him at odds with core American values, even as he holds the office of the President.

A useful exercise is to list three to five traits of Trump the narcissist that you can unlearn in your own life. Also, several of his businesses have benefited in cash and kind from his time in office. Is that true or false, and can you back it up with clear reasons? Finally, why is it vital for a sitting President to step away from any source of conflict of interest? How does such a conflict get in the way of doing the constitutional job?
